questions.title: Basic Inheritance Keyword
questions.description: Which keyword is used in Java to create a subclass that inherits from a superclass?
options: super
implements
inherits
extends
questions.explanation: In Java, the 'extends' keyword is used to declare that a class inherits from another class. For example, 'class Dog extends Animal' means Dog is a subclass of Animal. The 'implements' keyword is used for interfaces, not class inheritance. There is no 'inherits' keyword in Java. The 'super' keyword is used inside a subclass to refer to the parent class, not to declare inheritance.
hints: Think about the keyword that establishes an 'is-a' relationship between classes
This keyword literally means the subclass 'stretches out' from the superclass

questions.title: Is-a vs Has-a Relationship
questions.description: A Car class has a field of type Engine. What type of relationship does this represent?
options: Dependency relationship
Is-a relationship
Has-a relationship
Generalization relationship
questions.explanation: When a class contains a field (instance variable) of another class type, it represents a 'has-a' relationship. A Car 'has an' Engine. This is also known as composition or aggregation in UML. An 'is-a' relationship would use inheritance (extends), meaning the subclass IS A type of the superclass. Dependency is a weaker, temporary relationship. Generalization refers to inheritance hierarchies.
hints: Think about what it means for a Car to contain an Engine object
Does a Car 'have' an Engine or 'be' an Engine?

questions.title: Identifying Is-a Relationship
questions.description: Which of the following best represents an 'is-a' relationship?
options: A Car has an Engine
A Dog is a subclass of Animal
A Library contains Books
A Student uses a Calculator
questions.explanation: An 'is-a' relationship is modeled through inheritance. A Dog IS AN Animal, so Dog would extend Animal. 'A Car has an Engine' is a has-a relationship (composition/aggregation). 'A Library contains Books' is also a has-a relationship (aggregation). 'A Student uses a Calculator' represents a dependency relationship, which is the weakest form of coupling.
hints: An 'is-a' relationship means the subclass is a specialized type of the superclass
Which option describes a type hierarchy?

questions.title: UML Association Arrow
questions.description: In a UML class diagram, a simple solid line between two classes (without any special arrowheads or decorations) represents which relationship?
options: Association
Inheritance
Composition
Dependency
questions.explanation: A simple solid line in UML represents an association between two classes. Inheritance uses a solid line with a hollow triangle arrowhead pointing to the superclass. Composition uses a solid line with a filled diamond at the composite end. Dependency uses a dashed line with an open arrowhead. Association is the most general relationship, indicating that objects of one class are connected to objects of another class.
hints: The simplest line notation represents the most general relationship
Special arrowheads and decorations indicate more specific relationship types

questions.title: Method Overriding Basics
questions.description: What is method overriding in Java?
options: A class defines two methods with the same name but different parameters
A subclass provides its own implementation of a method already defined in its superclass
A method calls itself recursively
A method in a class is made private so subclasses cannot access it
questions.explanation: Method overriding occurs when a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method that is already defined in its superclass. The overriding method must have the same name, return type, and parameter list. Option B describes method overloading (same name, different parameters), not overriding. Option C describes recursion. Option D describes access restriction, which does not relate to overriding.
hints: Overriding means the subclass replaces the superclass behavior
The method signature must match exactly

questions.title: Super Keyword Purpose
questions.description: What is the primary purpose of the 'super' keyword in Java?
options: To create a new instance of the current class
To refer to the immediate parent class and access its members or constructor
To declare a class as a superclass
To prevent a method from being overridden
questions.explanation: The 'super' keyword in Java is used to refer to the immediate parent (superclass). It can be used to call the superclass constructor (super()), access superclass methods (super.methodName()), and access superclass fields (super.fieldName). It is particularly useful when the subclass overrides a method but still wants to call the original implementation from the parent class. The 'new' keyword creates instances, there is no keyword to 'declare' a superclass, and the 'final' keyword prevents overriding.
hints: Think about how a child class can communicate with its parent class
This keyword lets you access the parent's version of an overridden method

questions.title: UML Inheritance Notation
questions.description: In UML, how is inheritance (generalization) represented between two classes?
options: A solid line with a filled diamond at one end
A dashed line with an open arrowhead
A solid line with a hollow triangle arrowhead pointing to the superclass
A solid line with a filled triangle arrowhead pointing to the subclass
questions.explanation: In UML, inheritance (also called generalization) is shown as a solid line with a hollow (unfilled) triangle arrowhead pointing from the subclass toward the superclass. The direction of the arrow is important: it points from the specific (subclass) to the general (superclass). A dashed line with an open arrowhead represents dependency. A filled diamond represents composition. The triangle always points to the superclass, not the subclass.
hints: The arrow always points toward the more general class
The triangle arrowhead is hollow (not filled)

questions.title: Composition vs Aggregation
questions.description: What is the key difference between composition and aggregation?
options: In composition, the part cannot exist without the whole; in aggregation, it can
Composition is stronger than inheritance; aggregation is weaker
Aggregation uses a filled diamond in UML; composition uses a hollow diamond
Composition is only used with interfaces; aggregation is used with classes
questions.explanation: The key difference between composition and aggregation is the lifecycle dependency. In composition (strong has-a), the part cannot exist independently of the whole. For example, a Room cannot exist without a Building. In aggregation (weak has-a), the part can exist independently. For example, a Student can exist without a University. In UML, composition uses a filled diamond and aggregation uses a hollow diamond (option C has them reversed). Neither is related to interfaces specifically.
hints: Think about whether the contained object can survive if the container is destroyed
A Room in a Building vs a Student in a University

questions.title: UML Multiplicity Notation
questions.description: In a UML class diagram, the multiplicity notation '1..*' means:
options: Zero or one
Zero or more
Exactly one
One or more (at least one)
questions.explanation: The multiplicity '1..*' means 'one or more', indicating that there must be at least one instance but there can be many. The first number (1) is the lower bound and the asterisk (*) means unlimited upper bound. '0..*' or simply '*' means zero or more. '1' or '1..1' means exactly one. '0..1' means zero or one (optional).
hints: The format is 'lower_bound..upper_bound'
The asterisk (*) means unlimited

questions.title: Abstract Class Basics
questions.description: Which statement about abstract classes in Java is correct?
options: An abstract class cannot be instantiated directly
An abstract class must contain only abstract methods
An abstract class cannot have a constructor
An abstract class cannot have instance variables
questions.explanation: An abstract class cannot be instantiated directly using the 'new' keyword - you must create a concrete subclass that extends it. However, abstract classes CAN contain both abstract and concrete (non-abstract) methods. They CAN have constructors (which are called by subclass constructors using super()). They CAN have instance variables. Abstract classes serve as partial implementations that define a common interface and shared behavior for their subclasses.
hints: Think about what makes a class 'abstract' - it is incomplete
You need a concrete subclass to create objects

questions.title: UML Dependency Notation
questions.description: In UML, a dependency relationship between two classes is shown as:
options: A dashed line with an open arrowhead
A solid line with a hollow triangle arrowhead
A solid line with a filled diamond
A solid line without any arrowhead
questions.explanation: A dependency in UML is represented by a dashed line with an open arrowhead pointing from the dependent class to the class it depends on. Dependency is the weakest relationship between classes, indicating that one class uses another temporarily (e.g., as a method parameter or local variable). A solid line with a hollow triangle is inheritance. A solid line with a filled diamond is composition. A plain solid line is a simple association.
hints: Dependency is the weakest relationship, so it gets the 'weakest' line type
Dashed lines indicate a less permanent connection than solid lines

questions.title: Generalization Concept
questions.description: In object-oriented design, generalization refers to:
options: Adding more specific details to a class
Extracting common features from multiple specific classes into a shared superclass
Creating multiple instances of a class
Hiding the internal details of a class
questions.explanation: Generalization is the process of extracting common attributes and behaviors from two or more specific classes and creating a more general superclass. For example, if you have Dog, Cat, and Bird classes that all share 'name' and 'age' attributes, you can generalize them into an Animal superclass. The reverse process (creating specific subclasses from a general class) is called specialization. Option B describes specialization. Option C describes instantiation. Option D describes encapsulation.
hints: Think about moving from specific classes to a more general parent class
It is the opposite of specialization

questions.title: UML Multiplicity: Zero or One
questions.description: In a UML class diagram, the multiplicity '0..1' on an association end means:
options: Any number of instances is allowed
Exactly zero or exactly one instances, never more
At least one instance is required
The association is optional - zero or one instance
questions.explanation: The multiplicity '0..1' means that the association is optional: there can be zero or one instance of the associated class. This is commonly used to model optional relationships. For example, an Employee may have 0 or 1 ParkingSpot (some employees might not have a parking spot). Option B is essentially saying the same thing but less precisely. Option C describes '1..*'. Option D describes '*' or '0..*'.
hints: The lower bound of 0 means the relationship is not mandatory
The upper bound of 1 means at most one

questions.title: UML Composition Diamond
questions.description: In UML, a filled (solid black) diamond on an association line indicates:
options: Inheritance - an is-a relationship
Aggregation - a weak whole-part relationship
Composition - a strong whole-part relationship
Dependency - a uses relationship
questions.explanation: A filled (solid black) diamond in UML indicates composition, which is the strongest form of association. The diamond is placed at the 'whole' end of the relationship. In composition, the parts have the same lifecycle as the whole - if the whole is destroyed, so are the parts. A hollow (unfilled) diamond indicates aggregation (weaker whole-part). A hollow triangle arrowhead indicates inheritance. A dashed arrow indicates dependency.
hints: Filled means stronger, hollow means weaker
Composition is the stronger form of has-a relationship

questions.title: Simple Inheritance Code
options: Woof
Compilation error
Runtime error
questions.explanation: Even though the variable 'a' is declared as type Animal, the actual object created is a Dog (new Dog()). In Java, method calls are resolved at runtime based on the actual object type (dynamic dispatch/polymorphism), not the declared variable type. Since Dog overrides the sound() method, the Dog version is called, printing 'Woof'. This is a fundamental example of polymorphism in action.
hints: Consider what type of object is actually created with 'new Dog()'
Java uses dynamic dispatch for method calls - the actual object type matters

questions.title: Super Constructor Call
options: Compilation error
Car created Vehicle:Car
Car created
Vehicle:Car Car created
questions.explanation: When creating a Car object, the Car constructor is called. The first statement is super("Car"), which calls the Vehicle constructor with the argument "Car". The Vehicle constructor sets the type and prints "Vehicle:Car ". After the super() call completes, execution returns to the Car constructor which prints "Car created". The superclass constructor always runs before the rest of the subclass constructor. This is why the output is "Vehicle:Car Car created".
hints: super() must be the first statement in a constructor
The superclass constructor runs before the subclass constructor body

questions.title: Method Overriding with Super
options: I am a shape - specifically a circle
I am a shape
specifically a circle
Compilation error
questions.explanation: The Circle class overrides the describe() method from Shape, but uses super.describe() to call the parent class version first. super.describe() returns "I am a shape", and then " - specifically a circle" is concatenated to it. This pattern is common when you want to extend the behavior of a parent method rather than completely replacing it. The super keyword allows the subclass to reuse the parent's implementation while adding its own behavior.
hints: super.describe() calls the Shape version of describe()
The return value of super.describe() is used as part of a string concatenation

questions.title: UML Aggregation Example
questions.description: A University has multiple Departments. If the University is dissolved, the Departments can continue to exist independently (perhaps as independent research units). Which UML relationship best models this?
options: Composition (filled diamond at University end)
Aggregation (hollow diamond at University end)
Inheritance (hollow triangle at University end)
Dependency (dashed arrow from Department to University)
questions.explanation: This is an aggregation relationship because the parts (Departments) can exist independently of the whole (University). If the University ceases to exist, the Departments could survive as independent entities. Aggregation is shown in UML with a hollow (unfilled) diamond at the whole end (University). Composition would mean the Departments cannot exist without the University. Inheritance would mean a Department 'is a' University. Dependency would be too weak for this structural relationship.
hints: The key question is: can the parts survive without the whole?
Hollow diamond = weaker whole-part relationship (parts can exist independently)

questions.title: Composition Example
questions.description: A House has multiple Rooms. If the House is demolished, the Rooms also cease to exist. Which UML relationship best models this?
options: Association (plain line between House and Room)
Aggregation (hollow diamond at House end)
Composition (filled diamond at House end)
Dependency (dashed line from Room to House)
questions.explanation: This is composition because the Rooms cannot exist independently of the House. When the House is demolished, its Rooms are also destroyed - they share the lifecycle of the whole. Composition is the strongest form of association and is represented in UML with a filled (solid) diamond at the whole end (House). Aggregation would be wrong because it implies the parts can exist independently. A plain association is too weak to express the lifecycle dependency.
hints: Do the Rooms make sense without the House? Can a Room exist on its own?
Filled diamond = strong ownership with shared lifecycle

questions.title: Identifying Dependency
questions.description: Consider a method: void printReport(Printer printer) { printer.print(this.report); }. What type of UML relationship exists between the class containing this method and the Printer class?
options: Dependency - the class temporarily uses Printer
Composition - the class owns a Printer
Association - the class has a permanent link to Printer
Inheritance - the class extends Printer
questions.explanation: This is a dependency relationship because the class uses the Printer only temporarily as a method parameter. The class does not store a reference to the Printer as a field - it only uses it within the scope of the printReport method. Dependency is the weakest relationship, indicated by a dashed arrow in UML. It would be an association or composition if the Printer were stored as an instance variable. Inheritance would require the 'extends' keyword.
hints: The Printer is only used as a parameter, not stored as a field
Dependency means the class 'uses' another class temporarily

questions.title: Abstract Method in Abstract Class
options: This animal says: Meow
Compilation error - cannot call abstract method
Meow
Runtime error - abstract method has no implementation
questions.explanation: The Animal class is abstract and has an abstract method makeSound() and a concrete method describe(). The describe() method calls makeSound() internally. When we create a Cat and call describe(), the concrete describe() method from Animal runs, and when it calls makeSound(), Java's dynamic dispatch calls Cat's implementation which returns "Meow". This is the Template Method pattern - the abstract class defines the algorithm structure, and subclasses fill in the specifics.
hints: describe() is a concrete method in Animal that calls the abstract makeSound()
At runtime, the Cat version of makeSound() is used because the object is a Cat

questions.title: Constructor Chaining with Super
questions.description: What happens if a subclass constructor does NOT include an explicit call to super()?
options: A compilation error always occurs
The superclass constructor is never called
The compiler automatically inserts a call to super() (the no-argument constructor of the superclass)
The subclass constructor runs twice
questions.explanation: If a subclass constructor does not explicitly call super() or super(args), the Java compiler automatically inserts a call to the no-argument constructor super() as the first statement. This ensures that the superclass is always properly initialized. However, if the superclass does NOT have a no-argument constructor (it only has parameterized constructors), then a compilation error will occur because the implicit super() call would have no matching constructor to call.
hints: Java ensures the parent class is always initialized before the child
This implicit call is to the no-argument version of the parent constructor

questions.title: UML Object Diagram vs Class Diagram
questions.description: What is the main difference between a UML class diagram and a UML object diagram?
options: A class diagram shows classes and their relationships; an object diagram shows specific instances at a point in time
A class diagram is used in design; an object diagram is used only in testing
A class diagram shows inheritance only; an object diagram shows all relationships
There is no difference - they are two names for the same diagram
questions.explanation: A UML class diagram shows the static structure of a system: classes, their attributes, methods, and relationships. An object diagram shows a snapshot of the system at a particular point in time, displaying specific instances (objects) of classes and the links between them. Object names are underlined and written as 'objectName:ClassName'. Object diagrams are useful for illustrating examples and verifying that a class design works with concrete data. Both diagrams can be used in design phases.
hints: Class diagrams are abstract (types), object diagrams are concrete (instances)
Object diagrams show actual values of attributes, not just their types

questions.title: Inheritance and Field Access
options: Parent Child
Child Child
Parent Parent
Child Parent
questions.explanation: This question illustrates the difference between field access and method calls in Java. Fields are NOT polymorphic - field access is resolved at compile time based on the declared type of the variable. Since p is declared as type Parent, p.name refers to Parent's name field ("Parent"). The getName() method is inherited from Parent and returns this.name, which in the context of the Parent class refers to Parent's name field ("Parent"). Unlike methods, fields are not overridden - the Child class has its own separate name field that hides the parent's field. The output is "Parent Parent".
hints: In Java, field access is resolved at compile time, not at runtime
Fields are not polymorphic - they use the declared type, not the actual type

questions.title: Reading UML Multiplicity
questions.description: In a UML class diagram, there is an association between Order and OrderLine with multiplicity '1' on the Order side and '1..*' on the OrderLine side. What does this mean?
options: Each Order has exactly one OrderLine, and each OrderLine belongs to one or more Orders
Each Order has one or more OrderLines, and each OrderLine belongs to exactly one Order
Each Order can have zero or more OrderLines
Orders and OrderLines have no specific cardinality constraint
questions.explanation: Reading UML multiplicities: the multiplicity on the Order side ('1') tells us how many Orders each OrderLine is associated with - exactly one. The multiplicity on the OrderLine side ('1..*') tells us how many OrderLines each Order can have - one or more. So every Order must have at least one OrderLine, and every OrderLine must belong to exactly one Order. This is a common pattern for order systems: an order cannot exist without at least one item.
hints: Read the multiplicity near a class to understand how many of THAT class are involved
The multiplicity near OrderLine tells you how many OrderLines an Order can have

questions.title: Abstract Class vs Concrete Class
questions.description: Which of the following is true about abstract classes in Java?
options: A concrete class cannot extend an abstract class
All methods in an abstract class must be abstract
An abstract class cannot extend another class
An abstract class can have both abstract and concrete methods
questions.explanation: Abstract classes can contain a mix of abstract methods (declared without a body using the 'abstract' keyword) and concrete methods (with a full implementation). This is one of the key advantages of abstract classes over interfaces (in older Java versions). Abstract classes CAN extend other classes (abstract or concrete). Concrete classes CAN and often DO extend abstract classes - this is how the abstract methods get their implementations. Abstract classes serve as partial blueprints for subclasses.
hints: Abstract classes provide a mix of defined behavior and undefined behavior
Think of abstract classes as partially complete blueprints

questions.title: UML Association Navigability
questions.description: In a UML class diagram, if there is an arrow from class A to class B on an association line, what does this indicate?
options: A knows about B (A can navigate to B), but B does not necessarily know about A
B inherits from A
A is composed of B
B depends on A
questions.explanation: A navigability arrow on an association line indicates the direction in which the association can be traversed. If the arrow points from A to B, it means A has a reference to B and can access it, but B does not necessarily have a reference back to A. This is called a unidirectional association. In Java, this typically means A has a field of type B. A bidirectional association (arrows in both directions or no arrows) means both classes know about each other. This is different from inheritance arrows (hollow triangle) or dependency arrows (dashed line).
hints: The arrow shows which class 'knows about' the other class
In code, it means class A has a field or reference to class B

questions.title: Override Annotation
questions.description: What is the purpose of the @Override annotation in Java?
options: It is required for method overriding to work
It tells the compiler to verify that the method actually overrides a method in the superclass
It makes the method run faster by enabling compiler optimizations
It prevents the method from being overridden by further subclasses
questions.explanation: The @Override annotation is an optional but highly recommended annotation that tells the Java compiler to check that the annotated method actually overrides a method declared in a superclass or interface. If the method does not correctly override (e.g., the method name is misspelled or parameters are wrong), the compiler will produce an error. Without @Override, such mistakes would silently create a new method instead of overriding. The annotation is not required for overriding to work, does not affect performance, and does not prevent further overriding (that would be the 'final' keyword).
hints: It is a safety check, not a requirement
Think about what happens if you accidentally misspell the method name

questions.title: Inheritance Chain Constructor Order
options: C B A
A B C
C A B
A C B
questions.explanation: When creating an object of class C, the constructors are called from the top of the inheritance chain downward. C's constructor implicitly calls super() which calls B's constructor, which in turn implicitly calls super() calling A's constructor. A's constructor runs first printing "A ", then B's constructor prints "B ", and finally C's constructor prints "C ". Constructors always execute from the most general (top of hierarchy) to the most specific (bottom).
hints: The superclass constructor always runs before the subclass constructor
Think about it: the parent must be initialized before the child can use it

questions.title: Relationship Strength Ordering
questions.description: Which correctly orders UML relationships from weakest to strongest?
options: Inheritance → Composition → Aggregation → Association → Dependency
Association → Dependency → Composition → Aggregation → Inheritance
Dependency → Association → Aggregation → Composition → Inheritance
Dependency → Aggregation → Association → Composition → Inheritance
questions.explanation: The correct ordering from weakest to strongest coupling is: Dependency (temporary use) → Association (structural link) → Aggregation (weak has-a, parts can exist independently) → Composition (strong has-a, parts cannot exist without the whole) → Inheritance (is-a, tightest coupling since the subclass is bound to the superclass structure). Understanding relationship strength is crucial for good design: prefer weaker relationships when possible to reduce coupling between classes.
hints: Dependency is the weakest - it is just a temporary use
Inheritance is the strongest - the subclass is tightly coupled to the superclass

questions.title: Abstract Class Cannot Be Instantiated
options: Prints null
Runtime error - abstract method has no body
Prints 0.0
Compilation error - cannot instantiate abstract class Shape
questions.explanation: You cannot create an instance of an abstract class using the 'new' keyword. The line 'Shape s = new Shape()' will cause a compilation error. Abstract classes are incomplete - they may contain abstract methods without implementations. To use an abstract class, you must create a concrete subclass that provides implementations for all abstract methods, and then instantiate that subclass. You can still declare variables of the abstract type (Shape s), but you must assign them instances of concrete subclasses.
hints: Abstract classes are like incomplete blueprints - you cannot build from an incomplete plan
The error happens at compile time, not at runtime

questions.title: UML Class Diagram Reading
questions.description: In a UML class diagram, a class box is divided into three compartments from top to bottom. What does each compartment contain?
options: Class name, attributes (fields), methods (operations)
Class name, methods (operations), attributes (fields)
Package name, class name, attributes (fields)
Class name, constructors, attributes (fields)
questions.explanation: A UML class box has three compartments: the top compartment contains the class name (centered and in bold, italic if abstract). The middle compartment lists the attributes (instance variables/fields) with their visibility, name, and type. The bottom compartment lists the operations (methods) with their visibility, name, parameters, and return type. The standard visibility markers are + for public, - for private, # for protected, and ~ for package-private.
hints: The order goes from most general (name) to most specific (behavior)
Attributes describe what the class HAS, methods describe what the class DOES

questions.title: Identifying Relationship from Code
options: Composition - Students cannot exist without the Classroom
Aggregation - Classroom has Students, but Students can exist without the Classroom
Inheritance - Classroom extends Student
Dependency - Classroom temporarily uses Student
questions.explanation: This is an aggregation relationship. The Classroom has a collection of Students (has-a), but Students exist independently of the Classroom. A Student can exist without being in a particular Classroom. The Student objects are created elsewhere and added to the Classroom through addStudent() - the Classroom does not create the Students itself. If the Classroom is destroyed, the Student objects still exist. This distinguishes it from composition, where the whole creates and owns the parts.
hints: Students are added from outside the Classroom - they are not created by it
Can a Student exist without being in any particular Classroom?

questions.title: Super Field Access
options: Compilation error
questions.explanation: The Derived class declares its own 'value' field which hides (shadows) the 'value' field from Base. Inside printValues(), 'super.value' accesses the Base class field (10), while 'this.value' accesses the Derived class field (20). This demonstrates field hiding (not overriding - fields cannot be overridden in Java) and the use of 'super' to access hidden parent fields. Note that unlike method overriding, field hiding depends on the reference type, not the object type.
hints: super.value accesses the parent's field, this.value accesses the current class's field
Fields are hidden (shadowed), not overridden in Java

questions.title: UML Object Diagram Notation
questions.description: In a UML object diagram, how is an object typically represented?
options: :ClassName in italic with no attributes shown
ClassName.objectName in bold with methods listed below
Same as a class diagram box but with a dashed border
objectName:ClassName with the text underlined, and attribute values shown below
questions.explanation: In a UML object diagram, an object is shown as a rectangle with the object name and class name underlined in the format 'objectName:ClassName' (e.g., 'myDog:Dog'). The bottom compartment shows attribute values (not types), such as 'name = "Rex"'. If the object name is omitted, it can be written as ':ClassName' (an anonymous object). The underlining distinguishes object diagram elements from class diagram elements. Object diagrams do not show methods, as methods belong to classes, not individual instances.
hints: The underline is the key visual difference from class diagram boxes
Object diagrams show values, not types

questions.title: Complex Inheritance with Overriding
options: BA
AB
BB
AA
questions.explanation: Let's trace through: obj is of type C. Calling obj.display() invokes C's display(). C.display() calls super.display() which goes to A.display() (B does not override display()). A.display() calls info(). Due to dynamic dispatch, info() resolves to B's info() (since B overrides it and C does not override it further), returning "B". So super.display() returns "B". Then C.display() also calls info(), which again resolves to B's info() returning "B". The result is "B" + "B" = "BB".
hints: Trace which version of info() gets called - remember dynamic dispatch
super.display() calls A's display(), but A's display() calls info() polymorphically

questions.title: Abstract Class with Constructor
options: Runtime error
Truck with 6 wheels 6
Compilation error - cannot call abstract method in constructor
Truck with 0 wheels 6
questions.explanation: This is a tricky example of calling an overrideable method from a constructor. When super("Truck") is called in Truck's constructor, the Vehicle constructor runs. It sets type to "Truck" and then calls describe(). Due to dynamic dispatch, Truck's describe() is called. However, at this point, the Truck constructor has not yet executed 'this.wheels = 6', so 'wheels' has its default int value of 0. Thus describe() returns "Truck with 0 wheels". After the super constructor completes, 'this.wheels = 6' is executed, and then '6' is printed. This demonstrates why calling overrideable methods from constructors is dangerous.
hints: When does the super constructor run relative to the subclass field initialization?
What is the default value of an int in Java before it is explicitly assigned?

questions.title: Multiple Relationship Types in Code
options: One: Inheritance only
Two: Inheritance (Car → Vehicle), Association (Car → Engine)
Four: Inheritance, Composition, Dependency, and Aggregation
Three: Inheritance (Car → Vehicle), Composition (Car → Engine), Dependency (Car → Logger)
questions.explanation: There are three distinct relationships: (1) Inheritance: Car extends Vehicle (is-a relationship). (2) Composition: Car has a private Engine field that is created inside Car (new Engine()) - the Engine is created by the Car and cannot exist independently in this design. (3) Dependency: Car uses Logger only within its drive() method to call Logger.log(). Logger is not stored as a field - it is used temporarily, making it a dependency. There is no aggregation visible in this code (aggregation would require the parts to be passed in from outside).
hints: Look for extends (inheritance), fields (association/composition/aggregation), and method-only usage (dependency)
Is the Engine created inside the Car or passed from outside?

questions.title: UML Multiplicity Complex Scenario
questions.description: A UML class diagram shows: Professor (1..*) teaches (1..5) Course. What does this mean?
options: There are between 1 and 5 Professors and unlimited Courses
Each Professor teaches one or more Courses, and each Course has 1 to 5 Professors
Each Course is taught by one or more Professors, and each Professor teaches 1 to 5 Courses
Each Professor can have between 1 and 5 students in a Course
questions.explanation: In UML, multiplicities are read from the perspective of the opposite class. The multiplicity near Professor ('1..*') is read from Course's perspective: each Course is taught by one or more Professors. The multiplicity near Course ('1..5') is read from Professor's perspective: each Professor teaches between 1 and 5 Courses. This is a common source of confusion - the multiplicity is placed at the end of the association nearest to the class it counts. Always read multiplicity from the perspective of the class at the other end of the association.
hints: The multiplicity near a class tells how many of THAT class are involved per instance of the OTHER class
Read the multiplicity from the opposite end's perspective

questions.title: Preventing Method Overriding
questions.description: In Java, which approach prevents a method from being overridden by subclasses?
options: Declare the method as abstract
Declare the method as final
Declare the method as static and private
Use the @Override annotation
questions.explanation: The 'final' keyword on a method prevents subclasses from overriding it. A final method's implementation is guaranteed to be the one that is called. Declaring a method as abstract does the opposite - it requires subclasses to override it. While static methods cannot be truly overridden (they can be hidden), declaring a method as 'static and private' is not the standard way to prevent overriding and would change the method's behavior entirely. @Override only verifies that overriding is correct; it does not prevent further overriding.
hints: Think about which keyword means 'this is the definitive version'
The keyword literally means 'this is the final version and cannot be changed'

questions.title: Polymorphic Method Resolution
options: Animal Animal
Animal Dog
Dog Animal
Dog Dog
questions.explanation: Let's trace through: a.printType() calls Dog's printType() (dynamic dispatch). Dog.printType() first calls super.printType(), which is Animal.printType(). Inside Animal.printType(), type() is called - but since the actual object is a Dog, dynamic dispatch calls Dog's type(), which returns "Dog". So "Dog " is printed. Then back in Dog.printType(), type() is called directly, which also returns "Dog" (it is the same object). So "Dog" is printed. The full output is "Dog Dog". This demonstrates that even inside a super method call, the actual object type still determines which overridden methods are called.
hints: super.printType() calls Animal's printType, but type() inside Animal.printType still uses dynamic dispatch
The actual object type is always Dog, regardless of which class's method is executing

questions.title: UML Abstract Class Notation
questions.description: In a UML class diagram, how is an abstract class and its abstract methods typically indicated?
options: The class name is underlined and abstract methods are in bold
The class box has a dashed border and abstract methods have a # symbol
The class name and abstract method names are written in italics
The class box is shaded gray and abstract methods have no return type
questions.explanation: In UML, abstract classes and abstract methods are denoted by writing their names in italics. Alternatively, the stereotype <<abstract>> can be placed above the class name. Abstract method names are also italicized in the operations compartment. A dashed border is not used for abstract classes. Underlining is used in object diagrams (not class diagrams). The # symbol indicates protected visibility, not abstraction. These notation conventions help readers quickly identify which classes cannot be instantiated and which methods must be implemented by subclasses.
hints: The italic style signals 'not fully defined yet'
Alternatively, the <<abstract>> stereotype can be used

questions.title: Covariant Return Type Override
options: Animal Dog
Animal Animal
Dog Dog
Compilation error - return type mismatch
questions.explanation: This demonstrates covariant return types in Java. Dog's create() method overrides Animal's create() and returns Dog instead of Animal. This is valid because Dog is a subtype of Animal (covariant return type). When a.create() is called, dynamic dispatch calls Dog's create(), printing "Dog " and returning a new Dog object. Then result.getClass().getSimpleName() returns "Dog" since the actual object is a Dog. Covariant return types have been supported since Java 5 and allow a subclass to narrow the return type when overriding.
hints: Java allows overriding methods to return a more specific type (covariant return type)
Dog IS-AN Animal, so returning Dog where Animal is expected is valid

questions.title: Choosing Between Association Types
questions.description: A Library system has the following requirement: 'A Library catalog contains CatalogEntry objects. Each CatalogEntry references a Book. When a CatalogEntry is removed, the Book still exists in the system.' Which relationships should be modeled?
options: Composition between Library and CatalogEntry; Aggregation between CatalogEntry and Book
Aggregation between Library and CatalogEntry; Composition between CatalogEntry and Book
Composition for both relationships
Aggregation for both relationships
questions.explanation: The Library owns the CatalogEntry objects - if the Library's catalog is destroyed, the entries in it are also destroyed (they have no meaning outside the catalog). This is composition (filled diamond at Library). However, the Book exists independently of any CatalogEntry - removing a catalog entry does not destroy the book it references. This is aggregation (hollow diamond at CatalogEntry). This distinction is crucial in domain modeling: composition implies lifecycle ownership while aggregation implies a reference to an independently-existing object.
hints: Ask: does the CatalogEntry make sense without the Library? Does the Book make sense without the CatalogEntry?
Composition = shared lifecycle, Aggregation = independent lifecycle

questions.title: Super and This in Constructor
options: Prints Base then Base:10 then Derived:10
Prints Base:10 then Derived:10
Compilation error - this() and super() cannot both appear in the same constructor
Runtime error
questions.explanation: In Java, both this() and super() must be the first statement in a constructor. Since only one statement can be first, you cannot have both this() and super() in the same constructor. The Derived() constructor tries to call this(10) first and then super(), but this is illegal. You must choose one: either delegate to another constructor in the same class with this(), or delegate to a superclass constructor with super(). The other constructor in the chain will eventually call a superclass constructor.
hints: Both this() and super() must be the FIRST statement in a constructor
Only one call can be first - so you cannot have both

questions.title: UML Diagram to Java Code
questions.description: A UML class diagram shows: abstract class Shape with abstract method +area():double, class Circle extends Shape with attribute -radius:double and method +area():double, and a composition relationship from Drawing to Shape with multiplicity 1 on Drawing and * on Shape. Which Java code correctly implements this?
questions.explanation: The correct implementation follows the UML diagram precisely: Shape is an abstract class (not an interface) with an abstract area() method that is public (+) and returns double. Circle extends Shape (inheritance), has a private (-) radius field of type double, and implements the area() method publicly. Drawing has a composition (private ownership) relationship with Shape with multiplicity * (zero or more), modeled as a List<Shape>. Option B uses a concrete class and a single reference. Option C uses 'implements' with a class and has the wrong access modifier. Option D uses an interface instead of an abstract class.
hints: Pay attention to the + and - visibility markers in the UML diagram
Multiplicity * means zero or more, which requires a collection

questions.title: Multi-level Inheritance and Super
options: C A A
C B B
C C A
C B A
questions.explanation: Let's trace through each call: (1) c.name() returns "C" (C overrides name()). (2) c.superName() calls C's superName() which calls super.name(), i.e., B's name(), returning "B". (3) c.grandparentName() calls super.superName(), which calls B's superName(). B's superName() calls super.name(), which is A's name(), returning "A". Note that super.super.name() is not valid Java syntax - you cannot skip levels. Instead, C calls B's superName() which in turn calls A's name(). This chain correctly produces "C B A".
hints: super only refers to the immediate parent class
You cannot write super.super to skip a level in Java

questions.title: Abstract Class Partial Implementation
options: Compiles successfully - RegularPolygon implements perimeter() and Square implements area()
Compilation error - RegularPolygon must implement all abstract methods from Shape
Compilation error - Square must implement perimeter() again
Compilation error - abstract classes cannot have fields
questions.explanation: This code compiles successfully. Shape declares two abstract methods: area() and perimeter(). RegularPolygon extends Shape and is also abstract, so it is not required to implement all abstract methods - it only implements perimeter(). The area() method remains abstract (inherited from Shape). Square extends RegularPolygon and is concrete, so it must implement all remaining abstract methods. It implements area(), and perimeter() is inherited from RegularPolygon. This demonstrates how abstract class hierarchies allow incremental implementation across multiple levels.
hints: An abstract class does not have to implement all inherited abstract methods
Only concrete (non-abstract) classes must implement all abstract methods

questions.title: Designing Class Relationships
questions.description: You are designing a system where an Order contains OrderLines. Each OrderLine references a Product. When an Order is deleted, its OrderLines should also be deleted, but the Products should remain. An OrderProcessor class has a method that takes an Order as a parameter to process it. Which set of UML relationships is most appropriate?
options: Aggregation: Order → OrderLine, Composition: OrderLine → Product, Association: OrderProcessor → Order
Composition: Order → OrderLine, Aggregation: OrderLine → Product, Dependency: OrderProcessor → Order
Composition: Order → OrderLine, Composition: OrderLine → Product, Dependency: OrderProcessor → Order
Association: Order → OrderLine, Association: OrderLine → Product, Inheritance: OrderProcessor → Order
questions.explanation: The correct design uses: (1) Composition between Order and OrderLine because OrderLines are owned by the Order and are destroyed when the Order is deleted (shared lifecycle). (2) Aggregation between OrderLine and Product because Products exist independently and should remain when an OrderLine is deleted. (3) Dependency between OrderProcessor and Order because OrderProcessor only uses Order as a method parameter (temporary usage, not stored as a field). Option B incorrectly swaps composition and aggregation. Option C would incorrectly destroy Products when OrderLines are deleted. Option D uses inheritance, which makes no sense here.
hints: Consider the lifecycle of each object: which objects are destroyed when their parent is destroyed?
The OrderProcessor only uses Order in a method, not as a stored field

questions.title: Complex UML Diagram Interpretation
questions.description: A UML class diagram shows the following: abstract class Employee (italic name) with attributes -name:String, -salary:double and methods +getName():String, +calculatePay():double (italic). Two classes Manager and Developer both have generalization arrows pointing to Employee. Manager has an additional attribute -bonus:double. There is a composition from Department to Employee with multiplicity 1 on Department and 1..* on Employee. What can you conclude?
options: Department can have zero Employees; Employee can be instantiated; calculatePay() is fully implemented in Employee
Department must have at least one Employee; Manager and Developer must each implement calculatePay(); Employee cannot be instantiated directly
Manager and Developer are the same class; Department inherits from Employee
Employee is an interface; Manager and Developer implement it; Department aggregates Employees
questions.explanation: From the UML diagram: (1) Employee is abstract (italic name), so it cannot be instantiated directly. (2) calculatePay() is in italics, meaning it is abstract and must be implemented by concrete subclasses Manager and Developer. (3) getName() is not italic, so it is a concrete method inherited by subclasses. (4) The composition (filled diamond) from Department to Employee with multiplicity 1..* means each Department must have at least one Employee, and Employees cannot exist without a Department. (5) Manager and Developer have generalization (inheritance) arrows to Employee, making them concrete subclasses. (6) Manager has an additional bonus field for its pay calculation.
hints: Italic text in UML means abstract (both for class names and method names)
The multiplicity 1..* means 'at least one'
